FL.cra_notice_threshold: Required if a covered entity discovers circumstances requiring notice of more than 1,000 individuals at a single time: must notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices (§501.171(5)).
FL.consumer_notice_required: Methods limited to: written notice to the mailing address on file, or email notice to the email address on file (§501.171(4)(d)) — no telephonic-notice option appears in the Florida statute (unlike AZ/HI). Substitute notice permitted if cost would exceed $250,000, affected individuals exceed 500,000, or the covered entity lacks an email/mailing address for affected individuals — substitute = conspicuous website notice plus print and broadcast media notice in areas where affected individuals reside (§501.171(4)(f)). Required content: date/estimated date/date range of breach; description of personal information accessed or reasonably believed accessed; and contact information for the individual to inquire about the breach and their own affected information (§501.171(4)(e)). No credit-monitoring mandate found in the text. No special reduced-notice rule for breached online-login-credential-only data is stated for the individual-notice provisions (although 'personal information' does include username/email + password/security Q&A as its own category under (1)(g)1.b — the statute does not carve out a lighter notice method for that category the way AZ/DC do). Deemed-compliant safe harbor: notice given per the covered entity's primary/functional federal regulator's rules/guidelines satisfies both the individual-notice requirement and, if a timely copy is given to the Department, the Department-notice requirement (§501.171(4)(g)). No private cause of action (§501.171(10)). Civil penalties for violating the notice provisions: $1,000/day for the first 30 days, then $50,000 per subsequent 30-day period (or portion) up to 180 days, capped overall at $500,000 per breach (not per individual) (§501.171(9)(b)). Public-records/confidentiality protections apply to information the Department receives during an active investigation, and certain categories (personal information, forensic reports, security-weakness/proprietary information) remain confidential even after the investigation closes (§501.171(11)).

GA.statute_citation: O.C.G.A. §§10-1-911, 10-1-912 (Georgia Personal Identity Protection Act, breach-notification provisions; companion consumer/security-freeze provisions appear at §§10-1-913 to 10-1-915 but are not part of the breach-notice duty itself)
GA.notification_deadline: Notice in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, consistent with legitimate law-enforcement needs and measures necessary to restore system integrity — no fixed numeric day cap for the consumer notice appears in the text (§10-1-912(a)). Trigger is acquisition-based (unencrypted personal information 'was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired' by an unauthorized person); unlike AL/AR/FL, the Georgia text as provided does not contain an express 'reasonably likely to cause harm' qualifier or a documented no-harm exception process for withholding notice altogether. Law-enforcement delay: notice may be delayed if an LE agency determines it will compromise a criminal investigation, and made once LE determines it will not (§10-1-912(c)). Notable structural point: the breach-notice duty under §10-1-912 runs against 'information brokers' (entities in the business of furnishing personal information about individuals to nonaffiliated third parties for a fee) and 'data collectors' (state/local government agencies) — NOT against businesses generally with respect to their own customers' data, unless the business independently qualifies as an information broker (§10-1-911(2)-(3)). This is narrower in scope than most other states' breach statutes and should be flagged to the attorney. Processor/vendor duty: a person or business holding data on behalf of an information broker or data collector (without owning it) must notify the information broker/data collector within 24 hours following discovery, if personal information was or is reasonably believed to have been acquired by an unauthorized person (§10-1-912(b)) — notably faster than most other states' processor-notice deadlines.
GA.ag_notice_threshold: Confirmed — no provision in §§10-1-911 or 10-1-912 requires notice to the Georgia Attorney General. Georgia's breach-notification statute has no AG-notice duty of any kind.
GA.cra_notice_threshold: Required only if circumstances require notification of more than 10,000 Georgia residents at one time: must notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined by 15 U.S.C. §1681a) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices (§10-1-912(d)). This 10,000-resident threshold is notably higher than the 500/1,000 thresholds used by most other states.
GA.consumer_notice_required: Methods: written notice; telephone notice; electronic notice (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001); or substitute notice if cost would exceed $50,000, the affected class exceeds 100,000 individuals, or the information broker/data collector lacks sufficient contact information — substitute = email (if available), conspicuous website posting, and notification to major statewide media (§10-1-911(4)). No enumerated minimum notice-content list (e.g., breach date, description of information, contact info) appears anywhere in §§10-1-911/912 — the statute defines only acceptable notice *methods*, not required *content*, which distinguishes it from AL/AZ/DC/FL. No credit-monitoring mandate found. No special reduced-notice rule for breached online-login-credential-only data (Georgia's 'personal information' definition does not include a username/email + password category at all — it covers SSN, driver's license/state ID number, financial account/card number usable without further identifiers, and account passwords/PINs/access codes) (§10-1-911(6)). An information broker/data collector with its own notification procedures consistent with the article's timing requirements is deemed compliant (§10-1-911(4)). No GLBA/HIPAA exemption provision appears in §§10-1-911/912 — this absence should be flagged explicitly, since many comparable states include one. Good-faith employee/agent acquisition excluded from 'breach of the security of the system' (§10-1-911(1)).
GA.source_note: Extracted directly from ga-code-10-1-911-915-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use. Note: §§10-1-913 to 10-1-915 in the source file are consumer/protected-consumer security-freeze provisions, not part of the breach-notification duty, and were reviewed but are not the basis for the notification fields above.

HI.statute_citation: Haw. Rev. Stat. Chapter 487N, §§487N-1 to 487N-3 (private-business breach-notification provisions; §§487N-4 to 487N-7 are government-agency-specific reporting/administrative provisions, not directly applicable to private businesses)
HI.notification_deadline: Notice to affected persons without unreasonable delay, consistent with legitimate law-enforcement needs and measures necessary to determine sufficient contact information, breach scope, and system integrity — no fixed numeric day cap appears in the text (§487N-2(a)). Trigger: Hawaii's statutory definition of 'security breach' itself embeds a harm requirement — it is 'an incident of unauthorized access to and acquisition of unencrypted or unredacted records or data containing personal information WHERE illegal use of the personal information has occurred, or is reasonably likely to occur, AND that creates a risk of harm to a person' (§487N-1). (By contrast, unauthorized access to and acquisition of ENCRYPTED records together with the confidential key/process automatically constitutes a security breach with no separate harm showing required.) This is a harm-embedded definition (similar in effect to VA/UT-style harm triggers) rather than a separate exception process. Law-enforcement delay: notice delayed if an LE agency, in a request made in writing or contemporaneously documented (naming the requesting officer and agency), states that notice may impede a criminal investigation or jeopardize national security; notice then given without unreasonable delay once LE communicates it will no longer impede (§487N-2(c)). Processor duty: a business/government agency that maintains or possesses data it does not own/license must notify the owner/licensee immediately following discovery of the breach, consistent with the law-enforcement-delay provision — no fixed day-number specified (§487N-2(b)).
HI.ag_notice_threshold: Confirmed — Hawaii's private-business breach statute does NOT require notice to the Attorney General. Instead, if a business provides notice to more than 1,000 persons at one time, it must notify the State of Hawaii Office of Consumer Protection in writing, without unreasonable delay, of the timing, distribution, and content of the notice (§487N-2(f)) — analogous to states that designate a different office (not the AG) as the notice recipient. (Enforcement authority under §487N-3 is shared between the Attorney General and the executive director of the Office of Consumer Protection, but the §487N-2(f) notice recipient is specifically the Office of Consumer Protection.)
HI.cra_notice_threshold: Required on the same trigger as the Office of Consumer Protection notice: if a business provides notice to more than 1,000 persons at one time, it must also notify in writing, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notice (§487N-2(f)).
HI.consumer_notice_required: Methods: written notice to the last available address on file; electronic mail notice (only for persons with a valid email address on file who have agreed to electronic communications, consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001); telephonic notice (direct contact with the affected person); or substitute notice if cost would exceed $100,000, the affected class exceeds 200,000 persons, or the business/agency lacks sufficient contact information or consent for those specific individuals (partial substitute notice is allowed just for the affected subset lacking sufficient information) — substitute = email (if available), conspicuous website posting, and notification to major statewide media (§487N-2(e)). Notice must be 'clear and conspicuous' and must include: the incident described in general terms; the type of personal information subject to unauthorized access/acquisition; general acts taken to protect the information from further unauthorized access; a phone number for more information (if one exists); and advice to remain vigilant by reviewing account statements and monitoring free credit reports (§487N-2(d)) — this is advisory language only, not a mandated credit-monitoring service or specified duration. Deemed-compliant safe harbors: (1) a financial institution subject to the federal Interagency Guidance on Response Programs for Unauthorized Access to Customer Information and Customer Notice (2005) or 12 C.F.R. Part 748; (2) a health plan or healthcare provider subject to and compliant with HIPAA privacy and security standards (§487N-2(g)). No special reduced-notice rule for breached online-login-credential-only data — Hawaii's 'personal information' definition does not include a username/email + password category at all; it covers only SSN, driver's license/Hawaii ID card number, or financial account/card number + access code/password (§487N-1). Waiver of the chapter's provisions is void and unenforceable (§487N-2(h)). Businesses violating the chapter face civil penalties up to $2,500 per violation (enforceable by the AG or the Office of Consumer Protection's executive director) plus liability to the injured party for actual damages and possible attorney's fees; no such action may be brought against a government agency (§487N-3).
HI.source_note: Extracted directly from hi-hrs-487n-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use. Note: §§487N-4, 487N-6, and 487N-7 in the source file are government-agency-specific reporting/administrative provisions and were reviewed but are not the basis for the fields above, which reflect the private-business notification duty in §487N-2.

ID.notification_deadline: Harm-trigger: notice to affected Idaho residents required only if a good-faith, prompt investigation determines misuse of personal information has occurred or is reasonably likely to occur. No fixed day cap — notice must be given 'as soon as possible,' in 'the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,' consistent with law-enforcement needs and measures to determine breach scope/restore data integrity. Law-enforcement delay exception: notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency advises that it will impede a criminal investigation; once no longer impeding, notice must be given in good faith, without unreasonable delay, as soon as possible. A maintainer/non-owner of the data must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery' of a breach if misuse occurred or is reasonably likely to occur (and must cooperate, sharing breach-relevant information) — no separate numeric deadline given.
ID.ag_notice_threshold: Applies only to government 'agencies' (city, county, or state), not private commercial entities or individuals: when an agency becomes aware of a breach, it must notify the Idaho Attorney General's office within 24 hours of discovery — no numeric resident-count threshold, applies to any breach. State agencies also separately must report to the Office of the Chief Information Officer (Dept. of Administration) per Idaho technology authority policies. No AG-notice duty is imposed on private commercial entities or individuals anywhere in this text.
ID.cra_notice_threshold: None. No provision requiring notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies appears anywhere in §§28-51-104 through 28-51-107 — confirmed by full-text review.
ID.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written (to most recent address on file), telephonic, or electronic (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001). Substitute notice is available if cost would exceed $25,000, the number of Idaho residents to notify exceeds 50,000, or the entity lacks sufficient contact information; substitute notice = email (if addresses available) + conspicuous website posting (if a site is maintained) + notice to major statewide media. No specific notice-content/format requirements are prescribed, and no credit-monitoring or identity-theft-mitigation mandate appears in the text. No special rule for breached email/online-account login credentials only (personal information definition is limited to SSN, driver's license/state ID number, or financial account/card number plus required access code). Exemptions: an entity maintaining its own information-security-policy notice procedures consistent with the statute's timing is deemed compliant (§28-51-106(1)); an entity regulated by a state or federal primary/functional regulator that maintains procedures under that regulator's rules/guidance is deemed compliant if it follows those procedures (§28-51-106(2)) — this functions as a GLBA/functional-regulator-style safe harbor, though HIPAA is not separately named.
ID.source_note: Extracted directly from id-code-28-51-104-107-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

IL.statute_citation: 815 ILCS 530/1 et seq. (Personal Information Protection Act), specifically Secs. 10 (private data collectors) and 12 (State agencies)
IL.notification_deadline: Acquisition-trigger (no separate harm/misuse determination gates the duty to notify): private data collectors must notify affected Illinois residents 'following discovery or notification of the breach,' in 'the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,' consistent with measures to determine scope and restore data integrity/security — no fixed day cap for consumer notice under Sec. 10. State agencies (Sec. 12) are held to the same 'most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' standard for resident notice. Law-enforcement delay exception applies to both (written request from law enforcement required; notice must resume once no longer interfering). A data collector/maintainer that does not own the data must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery' if personal information was or is reasonably believed to have been acquired by an unauthorized person, and must cooperate (informing of breach date/nature and steps taken) — but is not required to disclose confidential business info/trade secrets or to itself notify residents.
IL.ag_notice_threshold: Private data collectors (Sec. 10(e)(2)): required if notice must be given to more than 500 Illinois residents from a single breach; must be sent 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' but no later than when consumer notice is provided (breach date may follow if unknown). Does not apply to HIPAA-covered entities/business associates in compliance with Sec. 50 (those instead notify AG within 5 business days of notifying HHS). State agencies (Sec. 12(e)): required if breach affects more than 250 Illinois residents; deadline is within 45 days of discovery or when consumer notice is given, whichever is sooner (extendable for good cause or law-enforcement request). Separately, State agencies directly responsible to the Governor with breaches affecting more than 250 residents (or instances of aggravated computer tampering) must also notify the Illinois DoIT Chief Information Security Officer and the AG within 72 hours of discovery (Sec. 12(g)).
IL.cra_notice_threshold: For State agencies only (Sec. 12(d)): if notice is required to more than 1,000 persons, the agency must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices (no requirement to disclose recipients' identifying information). For private data collectors under Sec. 10, no CRA-notification duty is stated anywhere in the text — confirmed by full-text review; Sec. 10 only requires that consumer-facing notices include CRA contact information as notice content, which is a different obligation.
IL.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, electronic (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001), or substitute notice if cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons, or the collector lacks sufficient contact information; substitute notice = email (if available) + conspicuous website posting + notice to major statewide (or, for geographically localized breaches, prominent local) media. Content requirements for SSN-type personal information: must include CRA toll-free numbers/addresses, FTC toll-free number/address/website, and a statement that the resident can get fraud-alert/security-freeze information from those sources. Special rule for breached login credentials only (username/email + password or security Q&A): notice may be electronic, directing the resident to promptly change the password/security question or take other protective steps for accounts sharing that username/password — and must NOT include the number of Illinois residents affected. No mandatory credit-monitoring/identity-theft-mitigation duration requirement found in the text. Exemptions: a data collector maintaining its own timing-compliant notification procedures is deemed compliant (Sec. 10(d)); covered entities/business associates in compliance with HIPAA/HITECH privacy and security standards are deemed compliant with the whole Act (Sec. 50), provided they also notify the AG within 5 business days of notifying HHS if HITECH notification to HHS was required.
IL.source_note: Extracted directly from il-pipa-815-530-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

KS.notification_deadline: Harm-trigger is built into the definition of 'security breach' itself (K.S.A. 50-7a01(h)): unauthorized access/acquisition must cause, or the entity reasonably believe has caused or will cause, identity theft to a consumer before the statute is even implicated (no separate post-discovery investigation step like Idaho's). Once triggered, notice to the affected Kansas resident must be given 'as soon as possible,' in 'the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,' consistent with law-enforcement needs and breach-scope/integrity-restoration measures — no fixed day cap. Law-enforcement delay exception: notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it will impede a criminal investigation, then must be given in good faith, without unreasonable delay, once no longer impeding. A maintainer/non-owner must notify the owner/licensee 'following discovery of a breach, if personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, accessed and acquired by an unauthorized person' — no separate numeric deadline stated.
KS.ag_notice_threshold: None. No provision in K.S.A. 50-7a01/50-7a02 requires notice to the Attorney General (or any other state office) as a breach-notice recipient — confirmed by full-text review. The Attorney General's only role in the statute is enforcement authority to bring a civil action for violations (except against insurance companies, where the Insurance Commissioner has sole enforcement authority) — neither role is a notice-receiving duty.
KS.cra_notice_threshold: Yes. If a breach requires notification of more than 1,000 consumers at one time, the notifying person must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined by 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices.
KS.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, electronic (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001), or substitute notice if cost would exceed $100,000, the affected class exceeds 5,000 consumers, or the entity lacks sufficient contact information; substitute notice = email (if available) + conspicuous website posting (if a site is maintained) + notice to major statewide media. No credit-monitoring or identity-theft-mitigation mandate appears in the text. No special rule for breached email/online-account login credentials only — the personal information definition covers only SSN, driver's license/state ID number, or financial account/card number plus required access code, not username/password combinations. Exemptions: an entity maintaining its own timing-compliant information-security-policy notice procedures is deemed compliant (§50-7a02(d)); an entity regulated by a state or federal primary/functional regulator following that regulator's established procedures is deemed compliant (§50-7a02(e)), though this does not relieve compliance with other state/federal privacy-protection law.
KS.source_note: Extracted directly from ks-stat-50-7a01-7a02-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

LA.statute_citation: La. R.S. §§51:3071-3075 (Database Security Breach Notification Law)
LA.notification_deadline: Acquisition-trigger by default (notice owed whenever personal information 'was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person'), but §3074(I) creates an affirmative harm-based exemption: notice is NOT required if, after a reasonable investigation, the entity determines there is no reasonable likelihood of harm to Louisiana residents (written determination and supporting documentation must be retained for 5 years from discovery, and sent to the AG within 30 days if requested in writing). Where notice is owed, it must be given 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay but not later than sixty days from discovery of the breach' — a hard 60-day cap. Law-enforcement delay exception: notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it would impede a criminal investigation; if delay pushes past the 60-day window (for law-enforcement reasons or because more time is needed to determine scope/restore integrity), the entity must give the AG written reasons for the delay within the 60-day period, and the AG may then grant a reasonable extension. A maintainer/non-owner of the data must notify the owner/licensee following discovery of a breach if personal information was or is reasonably believed to have been acquired by an unauthorized person — the 60-day cap in subsection E appears to apply to both the owner-to-resident (C) and maintainer-to-owner (D) notifications.
LA.ag_notice_threshold: The core statute (§§3071-3075) has no numeric AG-notice threshold in its own text, but a cross-referenced Louisiana Administrative Code provision (La. Admin. Code tit. 16, Pt. III, Ch. 7, §701, promulgated by the AG's office and included in this source file) requires written notice to the AG's Consumer Protection Section whenever notice to Louisiana citizens is required under R.S. 51:3074 — i.e., no numeric resident-count threshold; triggered by any breach requiring consumer notice. Notice to the AG is 'timely if received within 10 days of distribution of notice to Louisiana citizens'; failure to provide timely notice can draw a fine of up to $5,000 per violation, with each day of non-receipt treated as a separate violation.
LA.cra_notice_threshold: None. No provision requiring notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies appears anywhere in §§51:3071-3075 or the accompanying administrative rule — confirmed by full-text review.
LA.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, electronic (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001), or substitute notice if cost would exceed $100,000, the affected class exceeds 100,000 persons, or the entity lacks sufficient contact information; substitute notice = email (if available) + conspicuous website posting + notice to major statewide media. No credit-monitoring or identity-theft-mitigation duration mandate appears in the text. No special rule for breached email/online-account login credentials only (personal information covers SSN, driver's license/state ID number, financial account/card number plus required code, passport number, and biometric data — no username/password category). Exemption: an entity maintaining its own timing-compliant notification procedure as part of an information security policy is deemed compliant (§3074(H)). A violation of the Chapter is deemed an unfair trade practice under R.S. 51:1405(A) (§3074(J)), and a private civil action for actual damages is available for untimely notice (§3075).
LA.source_note: Extracted directly from la-rs-51-3071-3075-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

ME.notification_deadline: Trigger differs by entity type: information brokers must give notice 'following discovery or notification' of a breach once an investigation is conducted, with the statute's text not stating an explicit misuse-determination gate for brokers (§1348(1)(A)); other 'persons' must give notice only 'if misuse of the personal information has occurred or if it is reasonably possible that misuse will occur' (§1348(1)(B)) — a harm-trigger for non-brokers. For both, absent law-enforcement delay, notice must be made 'no more than 30 days after the person... becomes aware of a breach of security and identifies its scope' — a fixed 30-day cap once scope is known, and otherwise 'as expediently as possible and without unreasonable delay.' Law-enforcement delay exception: notice may be delayed for no longer than 7 business days after law enforcement determines notice will not compromise a criminal investigation. A third-party entity maintaining data on behalf of another (that it does not own) must notify the data owner 'immediately following discovery' if personal information was or is reasonably believed to have been acquired by an unauthorized person.
ME.ag_notice_threshold: Required whenever resident notice is triggered under subsection 1, but the recipient office varies: notice goes to 'the appropriate state regulators within the Department of Professional and Financial Regulation,' or, if the person is not regulated by that department, to the Attorney General instead (§1348(5)) — flagging this explicitly since it is NOT always the AG. No numeric resident-count threshold is stated; the duty tracks whenever consumer notice is required. No separate deadline stated beyond that.
ME.cra_notice_threshold: Yes. If a breach requires notification of more than 1,000 persons at a single time, the entity must also notify, without unreasonable delay, nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)); the notification must include the breach date, an estimate of the number of persons affected (if known), and the actual or anticipated date persons were/will be notified.
ME.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, electronic (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001), or substitute notice if the entity demonstrates cost would exceed $5,000, the affected class exceeds 1,000 individuals, or it lacks sufficient contact information for written/electronic notice; substitute notice = email (if available) + conspicuous posting on the entity's publicly accessible website + notice to major statewide media. No credit-monitoring or identity-theft-mitigation mandate appears in the text. No special carve-out procedure for breached login credentials specifically — the 'personal information' definition already includes 'account passwords or personal identification numbers or other access codes' as a qualifying data element (§1347(6)(D)), so a login-credential breach is handled under the ordinary regime rather than a distinct notice-content rule. PARTIAL — no statutory exemption (e.g., for GLBA- or HIPAA-compliant entities) appears anywhere in this text; its absence is confirmed by full-text review rather than assumed.
ME.source_note: Extracted directly from me-rev-stat-title10-1348-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

MA.notification_deadline: The harm element is built into the definition of 'breach of security' itself — the unauthorized acquisition/use must 'create a substantial risk of identity theft or fraud against a resident of the commonwealth' before the statute applies (no separate post-discovery misuse investigation step). Once triggered, notice must be given 'as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay' — no fixed day cap is stated anywhere in the text (unlike, e.g., Maine's 30-day cap). Notice may not be delayed on the ground that the total number of residents affected is not yet known; additional/updated notice must follow as soon as practicable once that information is available. Law-enforcement delay exception (§4): notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it may impede a criminal investigation and has notified the Attorney General in writing of that determination; once law enforcement advises the risk has passed, notice must be given as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay. A person/agency that maintains or stores (but does not own/license) resident data must notify the data owner/licensor 'as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay' upon knowing or having reason to know of a breach, and must cooperate (informing of the breach, its date, nature, and steps taken/planned) without being required to disclose confidential business information/trade secrets or itself notify residents.
MA.ag_notice_threshold: Required for any breach — no numeric resident-count threshold. The owner/licensor of the data must notify the Attorney General and the Director of the Office of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation (OCABR), in addition to the affected resident, 'as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay.' The AG/OCABR notice must include the nature of the breach, number of MA residents affected at the time of notification, identifying details about the breached entity and the person reporting, the type of personal information compromised, whether a written information security program is maintained, and steps taken/planned. A breaching entity must also file a report with the AG and OCABR Director certifying that its credit-monitoring services comply with Section 3A.
MA.cra_notice_threshold: The statute does not set a fixed numeric threshold (e.g., 500/1,000/10,000) for CRA notice. Instead, after receiving the AG/OCABR breach notice, the OCABR Director 'shall identify any relevant consumer reporting agency or state agency, as deemed appropriate,' and forward those names to the notifying entity, which must then notify the identified CRAs/state agencies 'as soon as practicable and without unreasonable delay.' Flagging explicitly: this is a Director-discretion mechanism rather than a numeric trigger, which is a notable departure from the Kansas/Maine/Illinois-style fixed-count CRA provisions.
MA.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, electronic (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001(c) and M.G.L. c. 110G), or substitute notice if the entity demonstrates cost of written notice would exceed $250,000, the affected class of MA residents exceeds 500,000, or it lacks sufficient contact information; substitute notice = email (if available) + clear/conspicuous homepage posting + statewide media publication/broadcast. Content is restricted rather than merely permissive: the resident-facing notice must include the resident's right to obtain a police report, how to request a security freeze (and that it is free of charge), and mitigation services to be provided — but must NOT include the nature of the breach or the number of MA residents affected (that detail goes only to the AG/OCABR). Mandatory credit-monitoring: if the breach includes a Social Security number, the entity must contract with a third party to offer affected residents credit monitoring at no cost for not less than 18 months — or not less than 42 months if the breaching entity is itself a consumer reporting agency; such contracts may not include reciprocal service-in-lieu-of-payment arrangements, and a resident cannot be required to waive their private right of action as a condition of the credit-monitoring offer. No special carve-out for breached email/login credentials only — the 'personal information' definition covers only SSN, driver's license/state ID number, or financial account/card number plus optional security code, not username/password combinations. Exemption: an entity following its own federal-law-based breach-response procedures (laws/rules/regulations/guidance/guidelines) is deemed compliant with the chapter, provided it also notifies the AG and OCABR Director of the breach as soon as practicable/without unreasonable delay, including the steps taken under that federal framework; noncompliance with the applicable federal framework voids the exemption.
MA.source_note: Extracted directly from ma-gen-laws-ch93h-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

MI.statute_citation: MCL §445.63 (definitions) and §445.72 (notice of security breach), Identity Theft Protection Act
MI.notification_deadline: Harm-trigger exemption: notice is not required if the entity determines the breach 'has not or is not likely to cause substantial loss or injury to, or result in identity theft with respect to' one or more Michigan residents (the entity must act with the care an ordinarily prudent person/agency would exercise in making that determination). Otherwise, notice must be given 'without unreasonable delay' — no fixed day cap. Two delay allowances: (a) time necessary to determine the breach's scope and restore database integrity (notice due without unreasonable delay after that work completes), and (b) a law enforcement agency's determination that notice would impede a criminal/civil investigation or jeopardize homeland/national security (notice due without unreasonable delay once law enforcement clears it). A maintainer/non-owner of the database must notify the data owner/licensor upon discovering a breach (same harm-trigger exemption applies) — falls under the general 'without unreasonable delay' timing of subsection (4), with no separate numeric deadline stated for the maintainer-to-owner notice specifically.
MI.ag_notice_threshold: None. No provision in §445.72 requires notice to the Attorney General or any state regulator as a breach-notice recipient — confirmed by full-text review. The Attorney General (along with a prosecuting attorney) appears only as an enforcement actor empowered to seek civil fines for knowing failures to notify, which is not a notice-receiving duty.
MI.cra_notice_threshold: Yes, with carve-outs. After providing consumer notice, the entity must notify each nationwide consumer reporting agency (15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) without unreasonable delay, including the number of notices given to Michigan residents and their timing — UNLESS (a) notice was required to 1,000 or fewer Michigan residents, or (b) the entity is subject to the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act (15 U.S.C. §§6801-6809). So the effective numeric threshold is >1,000 residents, subject to the GLBA carve-out.
MI.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be given by: (a) written notice to the postal address on file; (b) electronic notice, but only if the recipient expressly consented, or the entity has an existing business relationship with periodic email communications and reasonably believes it has a current email address, or the entity conducts business primarily online; (c) telephonic notice by a live representative (not a recorded message), requiring either the recipient's express consent or a fallback to written/electronic notice if no live conversation occurs within 3 business days of the initial attempt; or (d) substitute notice if cost would exceed $250,000 or the affected class exceeds 500,000 Michigan residents — substitute notice = email (if available) + conspicuous website posting + statewide media notification (must include a phone number or website for more information). Notice content must (for written/electronic notice) be clear and conspicuous and describe the breach in general terms, describe the type of personal information involved, describe (if applicable) protective measures taken, include a phone number for assistance, and remind recipients to remain vigilant against fraud/identity theft; the same content must be clearly communicated for telephonic notice. A public utility that sends monthly billing statements may combine notice with its billing statement instead of separate notice. No mandatory credit-monitoring/identity-theft-mitigation duration requirement appears in the text. No special carve-out for breached email/login credentials only — the 'personal information' definition covers only SSN, driver's license/state ID number, or financial/credit/debit account number plus required security code, not username/password combinations. Exemptions: financial institutions subject to (and examined for compliance with) the federal interagency guidance on customer-information response programs (or comparable NCUA guidance) are deemed compliant; entities subject to and complying with HIPAA (45 CFR parts 160 and 164) for breach prevention/notice are deemed compliant. False breach notices given with intent to defraud are a misdemeanor with escalating penalties for repeat violations; knowing failure to notify draws a civil fine of up to $250 per failure (aggregate cap $750,000 per breach across multiple violations).
MI.source_note: Extracted directly from mi-itpa-445.63-445.72-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

MS.notification_deadline: Default is acquisition-trigger with an affirmative harm-based opt-out: notice must be disclosed to all affected individuals 'without unreasonable delay,' subject to completion of an investigation to determine the incident's nature/scope, identify affected individuals, or restore data-system integrity — but notification is NOT required if, after an appropriate investigation, the entity 'reasonably determines that the breach will not likely result in harm to the affected individuals.' No fixed day cap is stated. Law-enforcement delay exception: notice must be delayed for a reasonable period if a law enforcement agency determines notice would impede a criminal investigation or national security and requests the delay; notice follows once law enforcement determines it will no longer compromise the investigation/security and so notifies the entity. A maintainer/non-owner of the data must notify the owner/licensee 'as soon as practicable following its discovery' if personal information was or is reasonably believed to have been acquired by an unauthorized person 'for fraudulent purposes.'
MS.ag_notice_threshold: NONE — confirmed explicitly. §75-24-29 contains no Attorney-General notification duty of any kind; subsection (8) gives the AG only enforcement authority (treating a violation as an unfair trade practice, with no private right of action created), not a notice-receiving role. The source file itself flags that secondary/compliance-tool sources circulating online claiming a '100 or more individuals' AG-notice threshold are NOT supported by the statutory text — that claim should be treated as incorrect.
MS.cra_notice_threshold: None. No provision requiring notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies appears anywhere in §75-24-29 — confirmed by full-text review.
MS.consumer_notice_required: Notice may be written, telephonic, electronic (if the entity's primary means of communication with affected individuals is electronic, or consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001), or substitute notice if cost would exceed $5,000, the affected class exceeds 5,000 individuals, or the entity lacks sufficient contact information; substitute notice = email (if available) + conspicuous website posting (if maintained) + notice to major statewide media (newspapers, radio, and television). No credit-monitoring or identity-theft-mitigation mandate appears in the text. No special carve-out for breached email/login credentials only — the 'personal information' definition covers only SSN, driver's license/state or tribal ID card number, or account/credit/debit card number plus required security code, not username/password combinations. Exemptions: an entity maintaining its own timing-compliant security breach procedures as part of an information security policy is deemed compliant; an entity maintaining procedures under the rules/regulations/guidelines of its primary or federal functional regulator (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §6809(2)) is likewise deemed compliant.
MS.source_note: Extracted directly from ms-code-75-24-29-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

MO.notification_deadline: Consumer notice: 'without unreasonable delay' — no fixed day cap. Pure acquisition-trigger (breach = unauthorized access AND acquisition compromising security/confidentiality/integrity of PI); no harm-trigger precondition to the duty itself, but notice is excused entirely if, after investigation or consultation with law enforcement, the person determines a risk of identity theft or other fraud is 'not reasonably likely to occur' (determination must be documented in writing and retained 5 years). Law-enforcement-delay exception: notice may be delayed if law enforcement (in a written or contemporaneously-documented request naming the officer/agency) states notice would impede a criminal investigation or jeopardize national/homeland security; notice then due without unreasonable delay after that determination lifts. Non-owner (person who maintains/possesses but doesn't own/license the data) must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery of the breach,' consistent with law enforcement needs.
MO.ag_notice_threshold: Required only if the person gives notice to more than 1,000 consumers at one time — must then notify the Attorney General's office 'without unreasonable delay' of the timing, distribution, and content of the notice. No AG notice duty at all below that 1,000-consumer threshold.
MO.cra_notice_threshold: Same >1,000-consumer trigger as the AG notice: must notify (without unreasonable delay) all nationwide consumer reporting agencies as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p), of timing, distribution, and content of the notice.
MO.consumer_notice_required: Methods: written notice; electronic notice (only for consumers with a valid email who've agreed to electronic communications, consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001); telephonic notice (direct contact only); or substitute notice if cost would exceed $100,000, OR affected class exceeds 150,000, OR insufficient contact info/consent (for only the affected subset lacking it), OR unable to identify particular consumers (for only that subset). Substitute notice must include ALL of: email notice (where an email address exists), conspicuous website posting, and notice to major statewide media. Required notice content: general description of the incident, type of PI involved, a phone number for further info/assistance (if one exists), CRA contact information, and advice to remain vigilant (review statements, monitor free credit reports). No credit-monitoring mandate found in the text. No special rule specifically for breached email/login credentials alone — Missouri's 'personal information' definition does not include a standalone username/email+password data element (unlike NV/NY). Exemptions: a person with its own notice procedures consistent with this section's timing is deemed compliant; a person regulated by a state/federal regulator's breach procedures is deemed compliant; financial institutions subject to and compliant with the Interagency Guidance (Fed/FDIC/OCC/OTS), NCUA regulations (12 CFR Part 748), or GLBA Title V (15 U.S.C. §§6801-6809) are deemed compliant.
MO.source_note: Extracted directly from mo-rev-stat-407.1500-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

NC.notification_deadline: Consumer notice: 'without unreasonable delay' — no fixed day cap. Pure acquisition/access-trigger (no harm-trigger precondition stated for notice duty itself). Law-enforcement-delay is mandatory language ('shall be delayed') if law enforcement (in writing, or via the business's contemporaneous written documentation naming the officer/agency) states notice would impede a criminal investigation or jeopardize national/homeland security; notice then due without unreasonable delay after that determination lifts. Non-owner business (maintains/possesses but doesn't own/license) must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery of the breach.'
NC.ag_notice_threshold: Two provisions, both to the Consumer Protection Division of the NC Attorney General's Office (not simply 'the AG' generically, but that office's division — confirmed by text): (1) subsection (e1) requires notice to the Consumer Protection Division 'without unreasonable delay' whenever the business gives notice to ANY affected person under this section — no numeric floor; notice must cover nature of breach, number of consumers affected, investigation/prevention steps, and timing/distribution/content of the consumer notice. (2) subsection (f) separately requires notice to the same Consumer Protection Division (paired with CRA notice) when notice is given to more than 1,000 persons at one time. Net effect: AG-office notice is required for essentially any reportable breach, not gated by a resident-count threshold.
NC.cra_notice_threshold: Required only when notice is given to more than 1,000 persons at one time — notify (without unreasonable delay) all nationwide CRAs as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p), alongside the Consumer Protection Division, of timing, distribution, and content.
NC.consumer_notice_required: Methods: written notice; electronic notice (valid email + consumer agreement to electronic communications, consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001); telephonic notice (direct contact); or substitute notice if cost would exceed $250,000, OR affected class exceeds 500,000, OR insufficient contact info/consent (for that subset), OR unidentifiable affected persons (for that subset). Substitute notice = ALL of: email notice (where available), conspicuous website posting, and notice to major statewide media. Notice must be 'clear and conspicuous' and include: general description of the incident; type of PI involved; general description of the business's protective acts taken; a phone number for further info (if one exists); advice to remain vigilant (review statements/monitor free credit reports); toll-free numbers/addresses for major CRAs; and toll-free numbers/addresses/websites for the FTC AND the NC Attorney General's Office plus a statement that the individual can get ID-theft-prevention info from these sources. No credit-monitoring mandate found. Notable definitional carve-out: 'personal information' for NC EXCLUDES electronic identification numbers, email names/addresses, internet account numbers, internet identification names, mother's maiden name, or a password — UNLESS that item would permit access to a financial account/resources — so a breach limited to email/login credentials alone (without financial-account access) is not a 'personal information' breach under this statute. Exemptions: a financial institution complying with the Federal Interagency Guidance (Fed/FDIC/OCC/OTS, 2005) or a credit union complying with the NCUA's parallel 2005 guidance is deemed compliant. Violation of this section is deemed a violation of the NC Unfair/Deceptive Trade Practices Act (G.S. 75-1.1); no private right of action absent actual injury; causes of action may not be assigned; any waiver of the Article is void as against public policy.
NC.source_note: Extracted directly from nc-gen-stat-75-65-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

ND.statute_citation: N.D. Cent. Code ch. 51-30, §§51-30-01 to 51-30-07 (breach-notice duty at §51-30-02)
ND.notification_deadline: Consumer notice: 'most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' — no fixed day cap. Pure acquisition-trigger (breach = unauthorized acquisition of unsecured computerized data; no harm-trigger precondition in the notice-duty text itself). Law-enforcement-delay exception (§51-30-04): notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines notice will impede a criminal investigation; notice must follow once that agency determines it will no longer compromise the investigation. Non-owner person maintaining the data must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following the discovery' if PI was or is reasonably believed to have been acquired by an unauthorized person (§51-30-03).
ND.ag_notice_threshold: Required whenever a breach exceeds 250 individuals (North Dakota residents) — the person must disclose to the Attorney General by mail or electronic mail. No separate deadline text distinct from the general 'most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' standard governing §51-30-02 as a whole. Below the 250-individual mark, no AG notice duty found.
ND.cra_notice_threshold: PARTIAL / CONFIRMED ABSENT — the full chapter (§§51-30-01 through 51-30-07) was reviewed and contains no provision requiring notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies at any threshold. North Dakota's breach statute has no CRA-notice duty.
ND.consumer_notice_required: Methods (§51-30-05) are limited to: written notice; electronic notice (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001); or substitute notice if cost would exceed $250,000, OR affected class exceeds 500,000, OR insufficient contact information. Note: unlike most peer states, North Dakota's method list does NOT include a telephonic-notice option. Substitute notice = ALL of: email notice (where an address exists), conspicuous website posting, and notice to major statewide media. No specific consumer-notice content requirements are set out in the statute (no itemized list of required disclosures, unlike MO/NC/NM). No credit-monitoring mandate found. No special rule specific to breached email/login credentials alone (ND's 'personal information' definition doesn't include a username/email+password combination as a category). Exemptions/alternate compliance (§51-30-06): a person with its own notice procedures consistent with this chapter's timing is deemed compliant; a financial institution, trust company, or credit union subject to, examined for, and compliant with the federal interagency guidance on breach response/customer notice is deemed compliant; and a HIPAA covered entity, business associate, or subcontractor subject to the breach-notice rules at 45 C.F.R. subpart D of part 164 is deemed compliant.
ND.source_note: Extracted directly from nd-cent-code-51-30-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

NV.statute_citation: Nev. Rev. Stat. §§603A.010-603A.290 (breach-notice duty at NRS 603A.220)
NV.notification_deadline: Consumer notice: 'most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' — no fixed day cap. Pure acquisition-trigger (breach = unauthorized acquisition that 'materially compromises' security/confidentiality/integrity of PI; no harm-trigger precondition in the notice-duty text). Law-enforcement-delay exception: notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it will impede a criminal investigation; notice must be made after that agency determines it will not compromise the investigation. Non-owner data collector must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery' if PI was or is reasonably believed to have been acquired by an unauthorized person.
NV.ag_notice_threshold: CONFIRMED ABSENT — the full 'Security of Information Maintained by Data Collectors' part (NRS 603A.010-603A.290) was reviewed and contains no provision requiring notice to the Attorney General (or any other state office) upon a breach. The Attorney General appears in this chapter only in an enforcement capacity (NRS 603A.290, injunctive actions) and NRS 603A.260 (deceptive-trade-practice classification), not as a recipient of breach notice. Nevada has no AG-notice duty for data breaches under this chapter.
NV.cra_notice_threshold: Required if notice is given to more than 1,000 persons at any one time — must notify (without unreasonable delay) any consumer reporting agency that compiles/maintains files on consumers nationwide, as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p), of the time and content of the notification.
NV.consumer_notice_required: Methods: written notification; electronic notification (consistent with the E-SIGN Act, 15 U.S.C. §§7001 et seq.); or substitute notification if cost would exceed $250,000, OR affected class exceeds 500,000, OR insufficient contact information. Substitute notice = ALL of: email notice (where an address exists), conspicuous website posting, and notice to major statewide media. No itemized consumer-notice content requirements are specified in NRS 603A.220 itself. No credit-monitoring mandate found. Special rule for login credentials: Nevada's 'personal information' definition (NRS 603A.040(1)(e)) DOES include a username/unique identifier/email address in combination with a password, access code, or security Q&A that permits access to an online account — so a pure credential breach (no SSN/financial data) can independently trigger the duty; no separate notice-mechanics carve-out for that category is specified beyond the general methods above. Exemptions: a data collector with its own notification policies/procedures consistent with this section's timing is deemed compliant; a data collector subject to and compliant with GLBA privacy/security provisions (15 U.S.C. §§6801 et seq.) is deemed compliant; this entire NRS 603A.010-290 part does not apply to a person licensed under NRS chapter 675 (installment loan/thrift companies) (subsection 7), nor to information handled under NRS 439.581-.597 (immunization information system). Also note: a related but SEPARATE PCI-DSS security (not notice) mandate applies to payment-card data collectors under NRS 603A.215, and a data-collector-vs-'operator' distinction routes online-sale-of-data breaches to the separate NRS 603A.300-360 statute, not this file.
NV.source_note: Extracted directly from nv-rev-stat-603a.010-290-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

NM.statute_citation: NMSA 1978 §§57-12C-1 to 57-12C-12 (Data Breach Notification Act; consumer-notice duty at §57-12C-6)
NM.notification_deadline: Consumer notice: fixed cap — 'most expedient time possible,' but not later than 45 calendar days following discovery of the breach (subject to the delayed-notice provisions of §57-12C-9). Acquisition-trigger with a harm-based exemption: notice is NOT required if, after an appropriate investigation, the person determines the breach does not give rise to a 'significant risk of identity theft or fraud' — i.e., the duty attaches on acquisition but can be excused by that risk assessment (not framed as a precondition the same way as VA/UT's 'misuse must occur or be reasonably likely'). Delay allowed if a law enforcement agency determines notice would impede a criminal investigation, or as necessary to determine the scope of the breach and restore system integrity/security/confidentiality (§57-12C-9) — no explicit 'must resume without unreasonable delay after' clause is spelled out (unlike most peer states), though the 45-day cap is stated as subject to §57-12C-9's delay grounds. Non-owner licensee/maintainer must notify the owner/licensee within the same 'most expedient time possible, but not later than forty-five calendar days' standard (subject to the same §9 delay grounds and the same significant-risk exemption).
NM.ag_notice_threshold: Required only if notice is issued to more than 1,000 New Mexico residents as a result of a single security breach — must notify the Office of the Attorney General (and major nationwide CRAs) 'in the most expedient time possible, and no later than forty-five calendar days' (subject to §9 delay grounds), including the number of NM residents notified and a copy of the notice sent to affected residents. Below 1,000 residents, no AG notice duty found.
NM.cra_notice_threshold: Same >1,000-NM-resident trigger as the AG notice: notify major CRAs that compile/maintain files on consumers nationwide, as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p), on the same 45-day-cap timeline.
NM.consumer_notice_required: Methods: U.S. mail; electronic notification (if the person primarily communicates with the resident electronically, or consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001); or substitute notification if cost would exceed $100,000, OR residents to be notified exceed 50,000, OR the person lacks a physical address/sufficient contact info. Substitute notice = ALL of: email notice (where an address exists), conspicuous website posting, AND written notice to the Office of the Attorney General AND major New Mexico media outlets (notably, NM's substitute-notice recipe routes a copy to the AG's office itself, unlike most peer states). Required notice content (§57-12C-7): name/contact info of the notifying person; list of PI types involved (if known); date/estimated date/date-range of the breach (if known); general description of the incident; toll-free numbers/addresses of major CRAs; advice to review account statements/credit reports; and advice on the recipient's rights under the federal Fair Credit Reporting Act. No credit-monitoring mandate found. No login-credential-specific notice mechanics (NM's 'personal identifying information' definition does not include a username/email+password category — SSN, driver's license, government ID, financial account+security code, or biometric data only). Exemptions: the entire Act does not apply to a person subject to GLBA or to HIPAA (§57-12C-8); a person with its own notice procedures consistent with this section's timing is deemed compliant (§57-12C-6(F)); the State of New Mexico and its political subdivisions are exempted entirely (§57-12C-12).
NM.source_note: Extracted directly from nm-nmsa-57-12c-1-12-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

NY.statute_citation: N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law §899-aa (Article 39-F, 'Notification of Unauthorized Acquisition of Private Information; Data Security Protections')
NY.notification_deadline: Consumer notice: 'most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay,' with a fixed outer cap — must be made within 30 days after the breach is discovered — except for legitimate law enforcement needs. Access-OR-acquisition trigger (broader than most peer states, which require acquisition; NY's 'breach' definition covers unauthorized access to, or acquisition of, computerized data). Notice exemption: not required if exposure was an inadvertent disclosure by an authorized person AND the business reasonably determines it won't likely result in misuse/financial harm (or emotional harm, for exposed online credentials) — must be documented in writing, retained 5+ years; if the incident affects more than 500 NY residents, the written determination must be given to the state AG within 10 days of the determination. Law-enforcement-delay exception: notice delayed if a law enforcement agency determines it impedes a criminal investigation; made after that agency determines it no longer compromises the investigation. Non-owner (maintains but doesn't own the data) must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately,' within 30 days following discovery, if PI was or is reasonably believed to have been accessed/acquired without authorization. Separate HIPAA-linked deadline: any covered entity that must notify HHS under HIPAA/HITECH must also notify the NY AG within 5 business days of notifying HHS — regardless of whether the info qualifies as 'private information' under this statute.
NY.ag_notice_threshold: Required for ANY breach that triggers resident notification — no numeric floor. Whenever any NY residents are to be notified, the person/business must notify the state Attorney General, the Department of State, AND the Division of State Police as to timing, content, distribution, and approximate number of affected persons, and must provide a copy of the notice template — made without delaying notice to affected residents. Additionally, if the person/business is a 'covered entity' under 23 NYCRR 500.1 (NY DFS cybersecurity regulation), notice must also go to the Department of Financial Services, per 23 NYCRR 500.17. Even where an exemption applies because notice was already given under GLBA/HIPAA/23 NYCRR 500/other state-federal regimes (so no additional consumer notice is required), notice to the AG, Dept. of State, Division of State Police, and CRAs is STILL required.
NY.cra_notice_threshold: Required if more than 5,000 New York residents are to be notified at one time — notify CRAs as to timing, content, and distribution of the notices and the approximate number of affected persons, without delaying notice to affected residents. Note: New York's CRA threshold (5,000) is notably higher than the common 500/1,000/10,000 figures seen in other states.
NY.consumer_notice_required: Methods: written notice; electronic notice (requires the recipient's express consent to receive notice electronically, plus a log of each notification kept by the notifying business — and a business may NOT condition establishing a business relationship or transaction on that consent); telephone notification (log required); or substitute notice if the business demonstrates TO THE STATE ATTORNEY GENERAL that cost would exceed $250,000, OR the affected class exceeds 500,000, OR the business lacks sufficient contact information (notably, NY requires that demonstration be made to the AG, not merely an internal determination). Substitute notice = ALL of: email notice where an address exists — EXCEPT if the breached information includes an email address plus a password/security-Q&A permitting online-account access, in which case the business must instead give clear, conspicuous notice delivered online when the consumer connects to the account from an IP/location the business knows the consumer customarily uses (a distinct, credential-specific substitute-notice mechanic unique among the states reviewed); conspicuous website posting; and notice to major statewide media. Required notice content (regardless of method): contact info for the notifying person/business; phone numbers/websites of relevant state and federal agencies providing breach-response and ID-theft-prevention info; and a description of the categories of information accessed/acquired, specifying which 'personal information' and 'private information' elements were involved. No credit-monitoring mandate found. Exemptions: if notice is already given to affected persons under GLBA Title V regs, HIPAA/HITECH regs, 23 NYCRR Part 500 (NY DFS cybersecurity rule), or other state/federal data-security rules and statutes as interpreted by the administering agency/courts, no ADDITIONAL consumer notice under this section is required — but AG/Dept. of State/State Police/DFS(if applicable)/CRA notice is still required as described above. This Article's provisions are exclusive and preempt any local law/ordinance/code purporting to impose inconsistent or more restrictive requirements.
NY.source_note: Extracted directly from ny-gbs-899-aa-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

OH.notification_deadline: Consumer notice: fixed cap — most expedient time possible but not later than 45 days following discovery/notification of the breach, subject to law-enforcement-delay and scope-determination/system-restoration needs. Genuine harm-trigger baked into the definition itself (not merely a post-hoc exemption): Ohio's 'breach of the security of the system' is defined as unauthorized access AND acquisition of PI that 'causes, reasonably is believed to have caused, or reasonably is believed will cause a material risk of identity theft or other fraud' to a resident — so the duty to notify does not attach on mere acquisition; a reasonable belief of material identity-theft/fraud risk must exist, comparable in structure to VA/UT's harm-trigger approach. Law-enforcement-delay exception: notice may be delayed if law enforcement determines it would impede a criminal investigation or jeopardize homeland/national security; made after that determination lifts. Non-owner custodian (holds data on behalf of/at direction of another person or governmental entity) must notify that person/entity 'in an expeditious manner' — no explicit day-cap stated for this leg (unlike the 45-day consumer-facing cap), and same harm-trigger condition (material risk of ID theft/fraud) applies to this notice duty as well.
OH.ag_notice_threshold: CONFIRMED ABSENT — the full text of §1349.19 was reviewed and contains no provision requiring notice to the Attorney General upon a breach. The Attorney General's only role under this statute is investigative/enforcement authority (bringing a civil action pursuant to R.C. §§1349.191 and 1349.192 for an alleged failure to comply), not as a recipient of breach notice. Ohio has no AG-notice duty for data breaches under this statute.
OH.cra_notice_threshold: Required if disclosure is required to more than 1,000 Ohio residents in a single occurrence of a breach — must notify (without unreasonable delay) all nationwide CRAs of the timing, distribution, and content of the disclosure given to residents; this CRA notice may never be used as a basis to delay the underlying consumer/owner notice.
OH.consumer_notice_required: Methods: written notice; electronic notice (only if the person's primary communication method with the resident is electronic); telephone notice; or a general substitute notice if the person lacks sufficient contact information, OR cost would exceed $250,000, OR the affected class exceeds 500,000 persons — substitute = ALL of: email notice (where available), conspicuous website posting, and notice to major media outlets whose combined readership/audience equals or exceeds 75% of Ohio's population (an unusual reach-based threshold, distinct from the flat 'major statewide media' language used elsewhere). SEPARATE small-business substitute-notice option: a business entity with 10 or fewer employees, where cost would exceed $10,000, may instead use: a paid newspaper ad (at least 1/4 page, published weekly for 3 consecutive weeks in the entity's local geographic area) + conspicuous website posting (if maintained) + notice to major media outlets in that local geographic area. No itemized consumer-notice content requirements are specified in the statute. No credit-monitoring mandate found. No login-credential-specific notice mechanics (Ohio's 'personal information' is limited to SSN, driver's/state ID number, or account/card number with a required security code/access code/password — no standalone username/email+password category). Exemptions: financial institutions, trust companies, or credit unions (or their affiliates) that are required by federal law to notify customers of a breach and are subject to examination by their functional federal regulator are EXEMPT from this section entirely; any person/entity that is a HIPAA 'covered entity' under 45 C.F.R. §160.103 is likewise EXEMPT from this section entirely (not merely deemed compliant — excluded from coverage). Any waiver of this section is void as against public policy.
