OK.statute_citation: 24 Okla. Stat. §§161-166 (Security Breach Notification Act), as substantially amended by Laws 2025, c. 406 (SB 626), effective January 1, 2026, applicable to breaches determined/noticed on or after that date
OK.notification_deadline: Consumer notice: 'without unreasonable delay' — no fixed day cap for the consumer-facing notice itself (contrast with the AG-notice leg below, which does have a cap), subject to measures necessary to determine breach scope/restore system integrity. Genuine harm-trigger baked into the 'breach' definition itself: requires unauthorized access AND acquisition of unencrypted/unredacted PI that 'causes, or the individual or entity reasonably believes has caused or will cause, identity theft or other fraud' to an OK resident — structurally similar to VA/UT/Ohio's harm-trigger approach (not a pure acquisition trigger). Law-enforcement-delay exception: notice may be delayed if law enforcement determines and advises that notice would impede a criminal OR civil investigation, or homeland/national security; notice then due without unreasonable delay after that impediment is lifted. Non-owner maintainer must notify the owner/licensee 'as soon as practicable following determination' if the entity reasonably believes PI was accessed/acquired by an unauthorized person.
OK.ag_notice_threshold: NEW as of the 2025 amendment (effective Jan. 1, 2026) — an individual/entity that must give consumer notice must also notify the Attorney General, without unreasonable delay but in no event more than 60 days after notifying affected OK residents. Two carve-out exemptions from THIS AG-notice duty specifically (the underlying consumer-notice duty still applies): (1) breaches affecting fewer than 500 OK residents are exempt from AG notice; (2) breaches at a credit bureau affecting fewer than 1,000 OK residents are exempt from AG notice (a separate, higher threshold specific to credit bureaus). Required AG-notice content: date of breach, date of determination, nature of breach, type of PI exposed, number of affected OK residents, estimated monetary impact (to the extent determinable), and any reasonable safeguards the entity employs. Personal information submitted to the AG is kept confidential under 51 O.S. §24A.12.
OK.cra_notice_threshold: CONFIRMED ABSENT — the full text of §§161-166 (including the 2025 amendments) was reviewed and contains no provision requiring notice to nationwide consumer reporting agencies. The only CRA-adjacent language is the credit-bureau-specific 1,000-resident exemption threshold carved out of the AG-notice duty (above) — that is an exemption from AG notice, not an independent duty to notify CRAs. Oklahoma has no CRA-notice duty under this statute.
OK.consumer_notice_required: Methods: written notice to the postal address on record; telephone notice; electronic notice; or substitute notice if cost would exceed $50,000, OR the affected class exceeds 100,000 residents, OR insufficient contact info/consent exists for the standard methods. Substitute notice consists of ANY TWO of the following three (an 'any two of three' structure, distinct from the cumulative 'all of the following' recipe used in most peer states): (1) email notice (where addresses exist), (2) conspicuous website posting, or (3) notice to major statewide media. No itemized consumer-notice content requirements are specified in the statute (content requirements are specified only for the AG notice, per above). No credit-monitoring mandate found. No login-credential-specific consumer-notice mechanics, though the 2025 amendment did add biometric data as a covered PI category. 'Reasonable safeguards' affirmative-defense/penalty structure (new in 2025): an entity that uses reasonable safeguards AND provides required notice is not subject to civil penalties (affirmative defense); an entity that provides required notice but did NOT use reasonable safeguards is not subject to the standard penalty cap but faces actual damages plus a flat $75,000 civil penalty (versus the standard cap of up to $150,000 per breach/series). Exemptions (deemed-compliant if the entity ALSO gives the AG notice required by §163(E)): financial institutions complying with GLBA and the federal Interagency Guidance on Response Programs; entities complying with the Oklahoma Hospital Cybersecurity Protection Act of 2023 or HIPAA; and entities complying with their primary/functional federal regulator's notification rules/procedures/guidelines. Enforcement is by the Attorney General or a district attorney (as an unlawful practice under the Oklahoma Consumer Protection Act), except violations by a state-chartered/state-licensed financial institution are enforceable exclusively by that institution's primary state regulator.
OK.source_note: Extracted directly from ok-stat-title24-161-166-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Note the source file itself carries a LOWER CONFIDENCE FLAG (Justia mirror, official oklegislature.gov/oscn.net not independently cross-checked in that fetch) — recommend a spot-check against the official state source before client use, in addition to the standard attorney sign-off gate.

PA.statute_citation: 73 P.S. §§ 2301 et seq. (Breach of Personal Information Notification Act)
PA.notification_deadline: General rule: notice 'without unreasonable delay' following determination of the breach — no fixed day cap for private entities/businesses (Section 3(a)). Government-sector deadlines are fixed: a State agency must notify affected residents within 7 business days following determination, with concurrent notice to the Office of Attorney General (Section 3(a.1)(1)); a county, public school, or municipality must notify within 7 business days, with notice to the county district attorney within 3 business days (Section 3(a.2)); a State agency under the Governor's jurisdiction must also notify the Governor's Office of Administration within 3 business days (Section 3(a.1)(3)). A State agency contractor must notify the affected State agency's chief information security officer 'as soon as reasonably practical,' no later than the timeframe specified in its contract (Section 3(a.1)(2)). A vendor (non-owner) that maintains/stores/manages data on behalf of another entity must notify that entity 'following discovery' by the vendor — no specific timeframe stated (Section 3(c)). Notice may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines in writing that it will impede a criminal/civil investigation or national/homeland security (Section 4).
PA.ag_notice_threshold: Yes. Concurrent notice to the Office of Attorney General is required when notice of the breach must be given to more than 500 affected individuals in Pennsylvania (Section 3(c.1)), with specified content (org name/location, breach date, incident summary, estimated total individuals affected, estimated PA-resident individuals affected). Entities subject to the insurance data security law (40 Pa.C.S. Ch. 45) are exempt from this AG-notice requirement (Section 3(c.2)).
PA.cra_notice_threshold: Yes. When an entity provides notification to more than 500 persons at one time, it must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in FCRA § 603 / 15 U.S.C. § 1681a) of the timing, distribution, and number of notices (Section 5).
PA.consumer_notice_required: Acceptable methods: written notice to last known home address; telephonic notice (if reasonably expected to reach the individual, clear/conspicuous, describes incident generally, verifies but doesn't solicit personal information, provides a phone number/website for more info); e-mail notice (only if a prior business relationship exists and a valid e-mail address is on file); electronic notice specifically for online-account credential breaches (directing prompt password/security-question change). Substitute notice permitted if cost would exceed $100,000, the affected class exceeds 175,000, or the entity lacks sufficient contact information; substitute notice = e-mail (if addresses known) + conspicuous website posting + notice to major statewide media. Credit monitoring/mitigation mandate: Section 5.4 — if breach involves SSN, bank account number, or driver's license/State ID number combined with name, the entity must, at its own cost, provide (1) access to one independent credit report and (2) 12 months of credit monitoring services (or notice of no-cost availability for 12 months), disclosed at the time of the breach notice. Special credentials rule: breaches of username/e-mail + password/security-Q&A for online accounts may be handled via electronic notice directing a prompt password change (Section 3(a.3), and definition of 'Notice' (3.1)). Exemptions: entities with their own compliant notification policy (Section 7(a)); financial institutions following the Federal Interagency Guidance (Section 7(b)(1)); entities following their primary state/federal regulator's procedures (Section 7(b)(2)); HIPAA/HITECH-compliant covered entities/business associates deemed compliant (Section 5.3).
PA.source_note: Extracted directly from pa-73-ps-2301-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

SC.notification_deadline: 'Most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay' — no fixed day cap. Trigger is a harm-based standard, not pure acquisition: notice is required only 'when the illegal use of the information has occurred or is reasonably likely to occur or use of the information creates a material risk of harm to the resident' (subsection (A), mirrored in the 'breach' definition at (D)(1)). Delay permitted if a law enforcement agency determines notification impedes a criminal investigation, until the agency determines it no longer compromises the investigation (subsection (C)). A non-owner maintainer of the data must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery' (subsection (B)) — no separate fixed cap given.
SC.ag_notice_threshold: No duty to notify the Attorney General as such. Instead, if a business provides notice to more than 1,000 persons at one time, it must notify, without unreasonable delay, the Consumer Protection Division of the Department of Consumer Affairs (not the AG's office) (subsection (K)).
SC.cra_notice_threshold: Yes. If notice is given to more than 1,000 persons at one time, the business must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notice (subsection (K), same trigger/sentence as the Consumer Protection Division notice).
SC.consumer_notice_required: Acceptable methods: written notice; electronic notice (if the person's primary communication method with the individual is electronic, or consistent with E-SIGN, 15 U.S.C. §7001); telephonic notice; substitute notice if cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000, or the person has insufficient contact information — substitute notice = e-mail (if addresses known) + conspicuous website posting + notice to major statewide media. No statutory credit-monitoring mandate found. No special rule specific to online-credential-only breaches found in this section. Exemptions: banks/financial institutions subject to and compliant with GLBA privacy/security provisions are entirely exempt from this section (subsection (I)); financial institutions compliant with the federal Interagency Guidance Response Programs are deemed compliant (subsection (J)); entities with their own consistent notification policy are deemed compliant (subsection (F)). Notable: creates a private right of action — injured residents may sue for damages (willful/knowing violation: damages; negligent violation: limited to actual damages), seek an injunction, and recover attorney's fees/costs if successful (subsection (G)); the Department of Consumer Affairs may also impose a $1,000-per-resident administrative fine for knowing/willful violations (subsection (H)).
SC.source_note: Extracted directly from sc-code-39-1-90-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

SD.notification_deadline: Fixed cap: disclosure to affected residents 'not later than sixty days from the discovery or notification of the breach,' unless a longer period is needed for legitimate law-enforcement needs (§22-40-20). If notice is delayed at law enforcement's request, it must then be made not later than 30 days after law enforcement determines notification will no longer compromise the investigation (§22-40-21). Trigger for consumer notice is acquisition-based ('was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired'), but a no-notice exception exists: an information holder need not disclose if, following an appropriate investigation AND notice to the attorney general, it reasonably determines the breach will not likely result in harm — this determination must be documented in writing and retained for at least 3 years (§22-40-20). The statute does not appear to contain a separate provision requiring a non-owner data processor/maintainer to notify the data owner — confirmed absent after full-text review; only 'information holder' (owner/licensor) obligations are addressed.
SD.ag_notice_threshold: Yes. Any information holder experiencing a breach exceeding 250 South Dakota residents must disclose to the attorney general by mail or electronic mail (§22-40-20). Separately, if the information holder invokes the no-harm exception to avoid notifying consumers, it must still give notice to the attorney general as part of that determination process, regardless of the 250-resident count.
SD.cra_notice_threshold: Yes, but no numeric threshold is specified — the statute requires notice to all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a) and any other nationwide credit bureau 'without unreasonable delay' whenever consumer notification under §22-40-20 is triggered at all (§22-40-24), unlike most other states which impose a numeric floor (e.g., 500/1,000/10,000) before CRA notice is required.
SD.consumer_notice_required: Acceptable methods: written notice; electronic notice (consistent with 15 U.S.C. §7001 as of Jan. 1, 2018, or if electronic is the information holder's primary communication method); substitute notice if cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000, or contact information is insufficient — substitute notice = e-mail (if addresses known) + conspicuous website posting + notice to statewide media. No statutory credit-monitoring mandate found. No special rule specific to online-credential-only breaches found (the 'protected information' definition covers username/email + password, but no distinct notice-method carve-out for it was found in the text). Exemptions: information holders regulated by HIPAA or GLBA that follow their primary/functional federal regulator's breach procedures are deemed compliant (§22-40-26); information holders with their own consistent notification policy are deemed compliant (§22-40-23).
SD.source_note: Extracted directly from sd-codified-laws-22-40-19-26-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

VT.notification_deadline: Most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, but not later than 45 days after discovery or notification of the breach, consistent with legitimate law-enforcement needs or measures to determine scope/restore integrity (subsection (b)(1)). Trigger is acquisition-based, but a documented no-misuse exception exists: notice is not required if the data collector establishes that misuse of PII/login credentials is 'not reasonably possible' and provides notice of that determination (with a detailed explanation) to the AG or Dept. of Financial Regulation (subsection (d)) — if facts later show misuse has occurred, notice must then be given. Law enforcement may request delay in writing (or the request must be contemporaneously documented if made otherwise) if it believes notice may impede an investigation or jeopardize public/national/homeland security (subsection (b)(4)(A)). A non-owner data collector (maintains/possesses PII it doesn't own/license) must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery' (subsection (b)(2)) — no fixed day cap given for that notice.
VT.ag_notice_threshold: Yes, and notably not limited to breaches above a numeric consumer-count threshold — notice to the Attorney General (or the Department of Financial Regulation for DFR-regulated entities) is required whenever the chapter's consumer-notice obligation is triggered (subsection (b)(3)). A preliminary notice (date of breach, date of discovery, preliminary description) is due within 14 business days of discovery or of consumer notice, whichever is sooner — unless the data collector had pre-registered written security policies with the AG, in which case notice/description is due before consumer notice is sent. A follow-up notice (number of Vermont consumers affected, copy of consumer notice) is required when consumer notice is sent. Special carve-out: if the breach is limited to unauthorized acquisition of login credentials, AG/DFR notice is required only if the credentials were acquired directly from the data collector or its agent (subsection (b)(3)(D)).
VT.cra_notice_threshold: Yes. If notice is provided to more than 1,000 consumers at one time, the data collector must notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notice (subsection (c)). This CRA-notice duty does not apply to a person licensed/registered under Title 8 by the Department of Financial Regulation.
VT.consumer_notice_required: Direct notice methods: written notice mailed to residence; electronic notice (with anti-phishing conspicuous warning language, or consistent with E-SIGN 15 U.S.C. §7001); telephonic notice (must be direct contact, not a prerecorded message). Substitute notice permitted only if the lowest cost among written/e-mail/telephonic notice would exceed $10,000, or contact information is insufficient — substitute notice = conspicuous website posting + notice to major statewide and regional media (no e-mail-notice component required, unlike most other states). Required notice content: incident description in general terms, type of PII involved, general protective actions taken, a toll-free (if available) phone number, advice to remain vigilant/monitor credit reports, and the approximate breach date (subsection (b)(5)). No specific credit-monitoring-duration mandate found. Special credential rules: breach limited to non-email login credentials — notify electronically or by other listed method, advising the consumer to change credentials for that and any other account using the same credentials (subsection (d)(3)); breach limited to e-mail-account login credentials — notice may NOT be sent through that e-mail account itself; must use another listed method or conspicuous online notice when the consumer connects from a known IP/location (subsection (d)(4)). Exemptions: HIPAA-covered entities (45 C.F.R. Part 164) deemed compliant if the breach is limited to PII specified in 9 V.S.A. §2430(10)(A)(vii) and the HIPAA breach rule (45 C.F.R. Part 164 Subpart D) is followed (subsection (e)); financial institutions following the 2005 Federal Interagency Guidance (banking) or NCUA guidance are exempt from this section, though DFR-regulated ones must still notify DFR promptly (subsection (g)). Any waiver of the chapter's provisions is void and unenforceable as against public policy (subsection (f)).
VT.source_note: Extracted directly from vt-9-vsa-2435-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

WA.notification_deadline: Most expedient time possible, without unreasonable delay, and no more than 30 calendar days after the breach was discovered, unless delayed at law enforcement's request or due to measures necessary to determine scope/restore integrity (subsection (8)). Trigger is acquisition-based, but notice is not required if the breach 'is not reasonably likely to subject consumers to a risk of harm' (subsection (1)) — an opt-out exception rather than an affirmative harm-trigger requirement. Law enforcement delay permitted if the data owner/licensee contacts law enforcement and law enforcement determines notice would impede a criminal investigation (subsection (3)). A non-owner person/business that maintains/possesses the data must notify the owner/licensee 'immediately following discovery' (subsection (2)) — no separate fixed cap.
WA.ag_notice_threshold: Yes. Notice to the Attorney General is required if notification must be issued to more than 500 Washington residents as a result of a single breach, due no more than 30 days after the breach was discovered, with specified content (number of WA consumers affected, types of PI involved, timeframe of exposure, summary of containment steps, a sample copy of the consumer notice) and must be updated if any required item was initially unknown (subsection (7)). HIPAA-covered entities must instead notify the AG per the federal HITECH Act §13402 timeliness requirements, notwithstanding the 30-day WA timeline (RCW 19.255.030(1)).
WA.cra_notice_threshold: No CRA-notice duty found in this chapter after full-text review. The only CRA-related requirement is that the consumer notice itself must include the toll-free phone numbers and addresses of the major credit reporting agencies (subsection (6)(b)(iv)) — that is consumer-notice content, not a separate obligation for the entity to proactively notify CRAs of the breach.
WA.consumer_notice_required: Acceptable methods: written notice; electronic notice (consistent with E-SIGN, 15 U.S.C. §7001); substitute notice if cost would exceed $250,000, the affected class exceeds 500,000, or contact information is insufficient — substitute notice = e-mail (if addresses known) + conspicuous website posting + notice to major statewide media. Notice must be in plain language and must include, at minimum: name/contact info of the reporting entity, the types of PI involved, a time frame of exposure (breach date and discovery date, if known), and toll-free numbers/addresses of major CRAs (subsection (6)). Special credential rules: breaches of username/password may be handled via electronic or e-mail notice instructing a prompt password/security-question change; if the breach involves login credentials for an e-mail account furnished by the entity, notice may NOT be sent to that e-mail address — another method must be used (subsection (4)(d)). No specific credit-monitoring-duration mandate found. Exemptions: HIPAA-covered entities deemed compliant re: protected health information if following HITECH §13402 (still must notify the WA AG per the federal timeline) (RCW 19.255.030(1)); financial institutions under OCC/FDIC/NCUA/Federal Reserve deemed compliant if following the interagency guidelines' customer-notice provisions, but must still notify the WA AG in addition to their primary federal regulator (RCW 19.255.030(2)); entities with their own consistent notification policy deemed compliant (subsection (5)). Notable: creates a private right of action — any consumer injured by a violation may bring a civil action for damages, and the chapter is also enforceable by the AG as an unfair/deceptive act under the state Consumer Protection Act (RCW 19.255.040) — flagged in the source file as unusual among breach statutes.
WA.source_note: Extracted directly from wa-rcw-19.255-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

WV.notification_deadline: Without unreasonable delay following discovery or notification of the breach — no fixed day cap. Harm-trigger required: notice is owed only when the breach 'causes, or the individual or entity reasonably believes has caused or will cause, identity theft or other fraud to any resident of this state' (§46A-2A-102(a), tracking the 'breach' definition in §46A-2A-101(1)). Law enforcement may delay notice by determining and advising in writing that it will impede a criminal/civil investigation or homeland/national security; notice then proceeds without unreasonable delay once law enforcement clears it (§46A-2A-102(e)). A non-owner entity that merely maintains the data must notify the owner/licensee 'as soon as practicable following discovery' (§46A-2A-102(c)) — no fixed day cap.
WV.ag_notice_threshold: No duty to notify the West Virginia Attorney General of a breach is found anywhere in §§46A-2A-101 to 105 after full-text review — the AG's only role under this article is enforcement of violations (§46A-2A-104), not receipt of breach notice.
WV.cra_notice_threshold: Yes. If an entity must notify more than 1,000 persons of a breach, it must also notify, without unreasonable delay, all nationwide consumer reporting agencies (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices (§46A-2A-102(f)) — but the entity need not disclose the names or other PII of the notice recipients to the CRA. This CRA-notice duty does not apply to an entity subject to Title V of the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act.
WV.consumer_notice_required: Acceptable methods: written notice to the postal address on file; telephonic notice; electronic notice (consistent with E-SIGN, 15 U.S.C. §7001); substitute notice if cost would exceed $50,000, the affected class exceeds 100,000, or contact information is insufficient — substitute notice must consist of ANY TWO of: e-mail notice (if addresses known), conspicuous website posting, or notice to major statewide media (notably requires only 2 of 3 elements, unlike most states' 'all of the following' formulations). Required notice content: to the extent possible, categories of information involved (SSN, driver's license/state ID, financial data); a phone number or website where the individual can learn what types of information the entity maintains and whether it held information about them; toll-free contact numbers/addresses for major credit reporting agencies and information on placing a fraud alert or security freeze (§46A-2A-102(d)). No specific credit-monitoring-duration mandate found. No special rule for online-credential-only breaches found in this statute (the PII definition does not include username/password/online-account credentials as a covered data element). Exemptions: entities with their own consistent notification policy deemed compliant; financial institutions following the Federal Interagency Guidance deemed compliant; entities following their primary/functional regulator's procedures deemed compliant (§46A-2A-103). The required notice is not to be considered a 'debt communication' under the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (§46A-2A-102(g)).
WV.source_note: Extracted directly from wv-code-46a-2a-101-105-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

WI.notification_deadline: Fixed cap: notice within a reasonable time, not to exceed 45 days after the entity learns of the acquisition of personal information (reasonableness assessed in light of number of notices required and communication methods available) (sub. (3)(a)). Trigger is knowledge-of-acquisition based, not a pure harm trigger, though a no-notice exception applies if the acquisition 'does not create a material risk of identity theft or fraud to the subject' (sub. (2)(cm)1.). Law enforcement may ask the entity not to notify for a period to protect an investigation or homeland security; the 45-day notification clock then begins at the end of that period, and the entity may not notify or publicize the breach except as authorized by law enforcement during that time (sub. (5)). Non-owner 'person storing' the data (who has not entered into a contract with the owner/licensor addressing this) must notify the owner/licensee 'as soon as practicable' after learning of unauthorized acquisition (sub. (2)(bm)) — no fixed day cap; if a contract with the owner already covers this, this statutory sub-duty is superseded by the contract terms.
WI.ag_notice_threshold: No Attorney General or other state-agency notice requirement of any kind is found in this section — confirmed explicitly by the source file itself (which was checked against the current 2025-26-updated statute text): notice runs only to affected individuals and, above a numeric threshold, to nationwide CRAs.
WI.cra_notice_threshold: Yes. If, as a result of a single incident, an entity is required to notify 1,000 or more individuals, it must, without unreasonable delay, notify all consumer reporting agencies that compile/maintain files on consumers nationwide (as defined in 15 U.S.C. §1681a(p)) of the timing, distribution, and content of the notices sent (sub. (2)(br)).
WI.consumer_notice_required: Notice must be given by mail or by a method the entity previously used to communicate with the subject; if the entity cannot with reasonable diligence determine a mailing address and has no prior communication method with the subject, it must use a method 'reasonably calculated to provide actual notice' (sub. (3)(b)). No substitute-notice option based on cost or affected-class size is provided in this statute — notably different from most other states' schemes. No specific content/format requirements are mandated for the notice itself beyond the general duty to notify; however, upon a recipient's written request, the entity must identify what personal information was acquired (sub. (3)(c)). No credit-monitoring mandate found. No special rule for online-credential-only breaches found (the definition of 'personal information' does not include username/password as a covered data element in Wisconsin, unlike many other states). Exemptions: entities subject to and compliant with GLBA privacy/security requirements (15 U.S.C. §§6801-6827), or their contractual counterparties with a breach-of-security policy in place, are exempt (sub. (3m)(a)); HIPAA-covered entities (as described in 45 C.F.R. §164.104(a)) compliant with 45 C.F.R. Part 164 are exempt (sub. (3m)(b)). Notably: failure to comply with this section is NOT itself negligence or breach of a legal duty but 'may be evidence of' negligence/breach of duty (sub. (4)); an annotation in the source text notes the statute creates no private right of action (Fox v. Iowa Health System, 399 F. Supp. 3d 780 (2019)). Local ordinances/regulations on this subject are preempted (sub. (6m)).
WI.source_note: Extracted directly from wi-stat-134.98-breach-notification.txt in the skill folder — grounded in actual statutory text, not general recollection. Still needs attorney sign-off before client use.

WY.notification_deadline: Notice must be given as soon as possible, in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, consistent with legitimate law-enforcement needs and measures necessary to determine scope/restore integrity — no fixed day cap. Notice is owed only after the entity conducts a 'reasonable and prompt investigation' and that investigation determines that misuse of PII has occurred or is reasonably likely to occur (§40-12-502(a)) — a harm/investigation-based trigger, not a pure acquisition trigger. Notification may be delayed if a law enforcement agency determines IN WRITING that notice may 'seriously impede' a criminal investigation (subsection (b)). A non-owner person maintaining data on behalf of another business entity must disclose the breach to that business entity 'as soon as practicable following the determination' that PII was or is reasonably believed to have been acquired by an unauthorized person; the parties may agree who provides the actual consumer notice (only one notice per breach is required), and if they cannot agree, the party with the direct business relationship with the resident must provide it (subsection (g)).
WY.ag_notice_threshold: No duty to notify the Wyoming Attorney General of a breach is found in this statute after full-text review — the AG's only role is enforcement authority to bring an action for violations and seek damages/other relief (subsection (f)), not receipt of breach notice.
WY.cra_notice_threshold: No CRA-notice-threshold provision requiring the entity to notify consumer reporting agencies of a breach is found in this statute after full-text review. (§40-12-501(a)(iii)-(iv) define 'consumer reporting agency' and 'credit report' only for purposes of the separate security-freeze provisions in §§40-12-503 through 509, which are not breach-notification provisions and were not part of this extraction.) The only CRA-related consumer-notice content requirement is that the notice must include a toll-free number from which the individual can learn CRA contact information (§40-12-502(e)(i)(B)) — that is content of the consumer notice, not an entity-to-CRA notification duty.
WY.consumer_notice_required: Acceptable methods: written notice; electronic mail notice; substitute notice if the person demonstrates cost would exceed $10,000 (Wyoming-based persons/businesses) or $250,000 (non-Wyoming-based businesses), OR the affected class exceeds 10,000 (WY-based) or 500,000 (non-WY-based), OR the person lacks sufficient contact information — substitute notice = conspicuous internet/website posting + notice to major statewide media (media notice must include a toll-free number for individuals to learn if their data was involved). Required consumer-notice content, clear and conspicuous, minimum: a toll-free number to contact the entity/its agent and to learn CRA contact info; types of PII involved; a general description of the breach incident; the approximate breach date if reasonably determinable; a general description of protective actions taken; advice to remain vigilant/monitor accounts and credit reports; and whether notice was delayed due to a law-enforcement investigation (subsection (e)). No specific credit-monitoring-duration mandate found. No special rule for online-credential-only breaches found (Wyoming's PII definition cross-references W.S. 6-3-901(b)(iii)-(xiv) rather than enumerating data elements inline — that cross-referenced statute was NOT fetched in this session, so the exact list of covered data elements, and whether it includes online credentials, is PARTIAL/unconfirmed pending review of W.S. 6-3-901). Exemptions: financial institutions/federal credit unions following GLBA (15 U.S.C. §6801(b)(3)) and the relevant interagency-guidelines appendices are deemed compliant if they notify affected Wyoming customers per those federal requirements (subsection (c)); HIPAA covered entities/business associates compliant with 45 C.F.R. Parts 160 and 164 are deemed compliant (subsection (h)).
